question: List and briefly describe the two primary types of requirements validation reviews.
expectedPoints: The two primary types are requirements reviews and prototype assessments.
Requirements reviews involve a formal meeting where stakeholders examine the requirements document for accuracy, consistency, and completeness.
Prototype assessments involve creating a working model (prototype) of the software to gather feedback and validate understanding with users.

question: Explain what a requirements modeling scenario is and describe the two main types used to represent them.
expectedPoints: A scenario is a narrative or sequence of steps that describes a specific use or interaction with a proposed system.
The two main types are use cases and user stories.
A use case is a more formal description of actor-system interactions, detailing main and alternative flows, preconditions, and postconditions.
A user story is a simpler, informal description of a feature written from the perspective of the user, following the format: 'As a [type of user], I want [some goal] so that [some reason].'

question: Compare and contrast the objectives and techniques of requirements validation and requirements verification. Discuss at least three specific validation techniques and explain how they help ensure the quality of the requirements specification.
expectedPoints: Validation asks 'Are we building the right product?' ensuring the requirements accurately capture stakeholder needs.
Verification asks 'Are we building the product right?' ensuring the design/code correctly implements the requirements.

Three key validation techniques are: 1) Requirements reviews - a formal manual check for errors and ambiguities. 2) Prototyping - creates an early model to elicit concrete feedback on look, feel, and functionality. 3) Test case development - writing tests based on requirements checks for clarity, completeness, and testability.
These techniques improve quality by identifying missing, incorrect, or unclear requirements early, reducing costly rework later in the project lifecycle.
